summary:
The Berlin Wall was constructed beginning on 13 August 1961, when the East German government, under Soviet pressure, began sealing the border between East and West Berlin. Initially consisting of barbed wire and improvised barriers, it was rapidly replaced by a concrete wall that eventually stretched 155 kilometers around West Berlin, supplemented by guard towers, trip-wire alarms, and a death strip maintained by the East German military.

The wall was built to stop the massive emigration of East Germans to the West through Berlin, which had served as the primary escape route for millions fleeing the communist system. Between 1949 and 1961, approximately 3.5 million East Germans — many of them skilled workers and professionals — had emigrated, creating a severe labor and brain drain crisis for the German Democratic Republic. The wall effectively ended this flow, trapping East Germans behind the Iron Curtain.

During the 28 years of its existence, at least 140 people were killed attempting to cross the wall, though some estimates place the total higher. Guards were ordered to shoot to kill those attempting to escape. The wall became the most potent symbol of the [[event:113|Cold War]] division of Europe, representing not just the division of Germany but the fundamental divide between liberal democracy and Soviet-style communism.

President Kennedy visited West Berlin in June 1963 and delivered his famous speech declaring solidarity with Berliners. The wall remained standing until November 1989, when it was opened and then demolished following the revolutionary events that preceded the [[event:2681|fall of the Berlin Wall]].
